title: Best Time to Buy and Sell Stock – One Pass Solution
content:
One of the most common beginner problems in Data Structures and Algorithms is Best Time to Buy and Sell Stock.

This problem is popular in coding interviews because it teaches an important pattern: tracking minimum values while maximizing profit in a single pass.

Problem Statement

You are given an array where:

prices[i] = price of a stock on day i

Rules:

  • You can buy one stock and sell one stock

  • You must buy before you sell

  • Return the maximum possible profit

  • If no profit is possible, return 0

Example:

Input: [7,1,5,3,6,4]
Output: 5

Explanation:

Buy at 1
Sell at 6
Profit = 5

Brute Force Approach

The most straightforward solution is:

  1. Try buying on every day.

  2. Try selling on every later day.

  3. Compute the profit.

  4. Keep the maximum.

Pseudo logic:

for i in range(n):
    for j in range(i+1, n):
        profit = prices[j] - prices[i]

Time Complexity:

O(n²)

This approach becomes too slow for large inputs.

Optimal Approach (One Pass)

Instead of checking all pairs, we can track:

  • The minimum price seen so far

  • The maximum profit

Key idea:

Profit = currentPrice - minimumPriceSeenSoFar

Every day we ask:

If I sell today, what is the cheapest price I could have bought earlier?

Step-by-Step Example

Array:

[7,1,5,3,6,4]

DayPriceMin PriceProfitMax Profit177002110035144431245615564135

Final Answer:

5

Algorithm

  1. Initialize:

    minPrice = ∞
maxProfit = 0

  2. Traverse the array

  3. Update minimum price

  4. Calculate today's profit

  5. Update maximum profit

Implementation (JavaScript)

function maxProfit(prices) {
    let minPrice = Infinity
    let maxProfit = 0

    for (let price of prices) {
        minPrice = Math.min(minPrice, price)
        let profit = price - minPrice
        maxProfit = Math.max(maxProfit, profit)
    }

    return maxProfit
}

Complexity Analysis

Time Complexity:

O(n)

Space Complexity:

O(1)

The algorithm scans the array only once, making it highly efficient.

Edge Case

If prices always decrease:

[9,8,7,6,5]

There is no opportunity to make profit, so the answer is:

0

Key Takeaway

This problem teaches a very powerful pattern:

Track Minimum → Calculate Difference → Track Maximum

This concept appears in many interview problems such as:

  • Maximum difference in arrays

  • Stock trading variations

  • Kadane-style optimization problems

Mastering this pattern will help you solve many array optimization problems efficiently.
